## [00:00 - 03:01]

So in this list I'm going to look at pricing. Now we've already touched on pricing a little bit in the onboarding versus complementary products but we're going to double down here and look at what is actually important with your pricing and what people often forget about. Now something I'm going to talk about now and give you run through a bit of an example is the difference between percentage margin and dollar margin. Now back in the day selling through distributors and things like that what you needed was percentage margin most of the time because you gave up a percentage and sold a product. Nowadays when it comes to direct to consumer brands what we actually need is dollar margin and we need dollar margin because we need the dollars in the product to be able to afford to buy that customer and this is a good example as to why. So we're going to look at our first product which has better percentage margin which is product X it's got a 40% margin it's got a $20 price tag and that product pricing stacks up like this. We've got a ship pit pack fee of $7.50 we've got a consumer acquisition cost only $2.50 we've got cost of goods of only $2 and they got a margin of $8. The problem with a product like this is that customer acquisition cost is very low and if we have to you know two, three, four times that we're going to run out of dollars in this product to actually make any money off the back end after we sell it. Now let's go to product Y. Product Y only has a 20% margin but $100 price tag. Now this is a much better situation because we've got room to move and let's look at how that stacks up. We've got a ship pit pack fee of $10 we've got a customer acquisition cost of $60 we've got cost of goods of $10 and we've got a margin of $20. Now this is something we can work with because of every one of these products we're selling we can afford to spend $60 on acquiring that customer. Now for an onboarding or flagship product this is what you need. The other product in this, the product X here, that is probably something you could make work for a complimentary or a cross sell or an upsell but for an onboarding product the way online is going you need something that you can afford to spend money going out showing your product to people and acquiring those customers and if you do have a product that has that kind of dollars built into it and afford to be able to spend that much money then you've got a business you can scale because you can spend that money they have to do one day going and getting new customers while still making some margin to keep the business running. So let's look at how both these sort of pricing structures look after 100 sales, 10,000 sales and 100,000 sales. So you've done 100 sales of product X with 40% margin at only $800 already product Y

## [03:01 - 06:03]

would be at $2,000 and 10,000 sales, product X is only at $80,000 sales, product Y is at $200,000 sales. So you've got 100,000 sales of both these products you've got product X at only $800,000 where you've got product Y already at $2,000,000. The thing here is it's not just the difference between these two pricing what it is is that there's absolutely no margin for error in one of these pricing models and in one of the pricing models product Y you've got margin for error you can afford to spend more and you've got something that you can really scale the business off. So I have to say with a lot of people I talk to over my time doing this, this is a really common mistake. People forget, one they forget that they need a portion of their, maybe the biggest cost that they're going to have is customer acquisition within their product. They're thinking about manufacturing, they're thinking about shipping but acquiring that customer may be your number one cost. So another mistake I see people make is that they think they've got enough margin in their product and they've got a good percentage margin but they may not have enough dollar margin and in the market the way things are going, cost of clicks, cost of CPMs, they're only going up. Now what you need to have a scalable product is that dollar margin in your product. If you've got that dollar margin in your product and it works in the market you can scale it. You may have a product that works in the market, you have good conversion rates, you have all these things but if you don't have enough dollars in there to show it to enough people it's still going to be very difficult to scale that product. And this is where you need to come up with your mix of complementary and onboarding products. Complementary products falling into this product X category will work and be fine and can still do very well for your business because effectively that cost of acquisition cost will be zero but an onboarding product like the product X can be very difficult. So aside from underestimating the cost of customer acquisition, something else I see people, founders, continuously get wrong is pricing strategy. Now often people think and you'll hear people say, oh you can always start high and go low later but I actually sort of disagree with that. I think starting high and then reducing your price isn't necessarily the best strategy and it can be a bit lazy. I think the best strategy is to try and get your price right but that's not always a point but starting high and then going low you can actually find yourself alienating a lot of your market and a lot of your key customers especially your early customers, the ones that seem to support you with everything you do. Now what I'd rather do I think is start the price low and then if it's not working for you push that price up higher because all the new customers that are coming to you at that time, they don't necessarily know your price used to be lower and all the customers that got that deal at the start that you sold to that supported you when you first started and they see you go up, they're like, huh, I got in at a deal, I got a steal. So starting low and going high necessarily isn't as hard as what people think.

## [06:03 - 06:18]

Starting high and going low can be alienating, it's some of your best customers. So as an action item for this lesson use the workbook and make a bar graph of your products to see how your pricing stack up and how much you have to work with. Do that now and I'll see you in the next lesson.
